item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woot woot!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed the picture here with Elogee's help to be CC compliant!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: That's a really good inclusion! Make the change, bud.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Go figure we had an SCP article about set of syntax that causes memetic shenanigans, and that artifacts from that language can be found all around the world. SCP-2518 mentions that the people living with in it (a gorge within northern Pakistan) has a language with potential origins from SCP-1390, so there is a good chance 1390 artifacts might be found in the region. I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1390 is a grammatical structure only exhibited in a dead language of the ████████ civilization of the island of ████. While records indicate no mass migration, artifacts containing instances of SCP-1390, known as SCP-1390-1, have been found in such varied areas as the Mongolian steppes, the Apennines, and the coast of Brazil. Research suggests that [DATA EXPUNGED]. (See Document-1390-A with Level 5 Security Clearance.) SCP-1390-1 objects resemble period art created by contemporaneous Mediterranean civilizations. No SCP-1390-1 objects are anomalous themselves and can therefore be classified as Safe.

To this:

SCP-1390 is a grammatical structure only exhibited in a dead language of the ████████ civilization of the island of ████. While records indicate no mass migration, artifacts containing instances of SCP-1390, known as SCP-1390-1, have been found in such varied areas as the Mongolian steppes, the Hindu Kush of northern Pakistan, the Apennines, and the coast of Brazil. Research suggests that [DATA EXPUNGED]. (See Document-1390-A with Level 5 Security Clearance.) SCP-1390-1 objects resemble period art created by contemporaneous Mediterranean civilizations. No SCP-1390-1 objects are anomalous themselves and can therefore be classified as Safe.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Being a language nut, I really liked and thoroughly enjoyed reading this skip. I was slightly annoyed by the xenophobic case, as I have difficulty imagining it practically, but I like the concept well enough. To me though, it seemed more like a form of clusivity than an actual case, as clusivity actively makes language uses distinguish between 'you' and 'us'.

I like the temporal and dimensional cases though. Don't really get how dimensional would work (as in, I'd really like to see an example of it!), but temporal is familiar enough to me (through my native language) and also fits well with the overall theme. I don't know if you're aware on an actual temporal case, but it's present if a few Uralic languages, both to define a specific point in time as well as frequency of occurrence.

Anyways: +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only gripe is that there's apparent inconsistency in the number of the letters comprising the (persumably adjectival form of the) name of the civilization. Sometimes it's 8 letters, other times it's 10. Which one is it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- Cognitohazard Warning!

This.

Why? There's no anomalous structure there. If there indeed was an anomalous structure then almost all non basic english language would be contaminated. It makes no sense.

Upvoted because awesome, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always been a sucker for language SCPs. +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i'm going to totally pretend that yes that was totally intended it was my master game all along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this one.

One tiny thing - the last paragraph of the containment procedures was missing a full stop. I've put that in, but was that deliberate? To make people notice it's missing and then think Oh God have I been infected?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
these people get angry about bad grammar?

an infected of any stage will, if presented to any social online-platform, [DATA EXPUNGED] resulting in a gigantic beam of light with[DATA EXPUNGED] flying towards each member. these entities are screaming in "pure rage" as reported by witnesses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mebbeh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have I ever told you that I love you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really, really enjoy this. I love SCPs that suggest things going on behind the scenes that aren't presented in the article and left up to the reader, and this has an interesting effect and implied background to boot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You are totally right about that, and I should change it at some point. The part about being boring, I cannot really help.

Edit: Actually I'm looking it up, and it says that some authors use "grammatical gender" to mean "noun class." It doesn't seem that one is more right than the other?

And really the fact that they have two noun classes isn't so much an anomaly, as I wanted the existence of those two other noun classes (and the fact that they only exist to refer to people infected by the grammar) to hint at this kind of… changing and becoming. Not so much that it's weird to have more genders, in and of itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As soon as I read that it was a memetic grammar structure, I got all set to upvote. Then I read the rest of it and goddamn was it boring. Plus

SCP-1390, however, contains two unknown genders, differing from the neuter, masculine, and feminine of most languages, but their role in the language is not well understood.

NO. That is not how lingusitic gender works, at all. First of all, you are thinking of the term "noun class", not gender. Gender is a subset of noun class, which generally refers to a type of noun class that categorizes by masculine/feminine, sometimes neuter, and variations of them. A language with five noun classes is not some crazy fucking anomaly. It's just slightly different than most Indo-European languages that us native English speakers would know about. This by itself would have been enough for me to downvote, but the rest of the article was just dull.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That totally isn't stupid. My answer is that you (the reader) don't have clearance to view the Documents. From an authorial standpoint, I kinda used them to link together clues about what was actually happening (and because I thought there'd need to be some Documents going into further detail about some of the stuff in there).

Short answer: I didn't write them but used them to imply greater study than people would want to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may sound stupid for asking, but where are the documents for this SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those aren't memtics though. Those are space candies. Memtics are different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd accept this as a dare, but I already wrote SCP-1916 a few months ago.

So, challenge preemptively fulfilled. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna start calling the amnesiacs memtics now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^ This! It should be the next article somebody writes. Seriously, there are so many around these days that are just awful!

Oh, upvote, freaking loved this one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Brought to you by Dr. Wondertainment! There's also a black memtic that does [DATA EXPUNGED].

(see because it's a dark memtic, it's evil)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, in pretty much every language but English, dude, there's a thing called grammatical gender. Here's a link to a better explanation than I can or will give: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grammatical_gender Also, grammatical structure is a little bit more than subject-verb agreement, dude. It can change the way entire people view the world. It's the structure to which we apply our experiences, and some of them are vastly different. Certain cultures don't have a past or future tense. Can you imagine what that would do to the way they perceive time? Stuff like that.

And, like I've been saying, it is pretty obvious what happens in Stage 3 infection. It pretty much goes out to say it directly if you put what few puzzle pieces I have together.

Edit: Also yeah, people make every language, but usually one dude doesn't make an entire language, with its own vocabulary and grammatical structure, by himself. Languages aren't manmade in the sense of Esperanto. They have a natural lifecycle and change and shift in certain ways as people forget grammar rules and add in new ones. Sometimes, vocabulary falls out of popularity, and certain words start meaning different stuff. That's totally different with Esperanto, which is stagnant. It's manmade in the same sense the Tolkien's Elvish is manmade. But I wouldn't say that English is manmade in the same way as Elvish.

Edit 2: I probably should mention the word memetic in there. At least once, huh? Good call on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, yeah, there's an idea. Let's expose an SCP that may or may not be the literal God, but who at the very least can walk through every barrier we have like it literally wasn't there, to another SCP that turns it into a grammar fascist. What could go wrong?

;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm…extremely confused.

Throughout half the article, I was maybe 80% sure whether or not the subject of the sentence I was reading was SCP-1390, a grammatical structure, or the language wherein SCP-1390 can be naturally used. Sentences like this:

SCP-1390, however, contains two unknown genders, differing from the neuter, masculine, and feminine of most languages, but their role in the language is not well understood.

Okay. SCP-1390 is NOT a language, right? It's very specifically mentioned that the language itself is NOT anomalous. SCP-1390 is a grammatical structure. To me, "grammatical structure" is something like subject-verb-object. How does stuff like that have a gender?

(also something that bugged me…Esperanto as a "man-made language." When I google the term, yes, I get a reference to Esperanto, but still, it doesn't make any sense because "man" made all the OTHER languages as well! Including the one I'm typing in! So…)

The containment procedures HINT that it's a memetic agent, or cognitohazard, whichever one you want I'm not in this for semantics bla bla, but…it doesn't outright SAY it. Which is terribly uninformative of you're in a containment breach and you need information now.

You don't actually tell us what happens in a Stage 3 transformation. I can only make plausible guesses. I don't actually know. Whatever I can think of, it doesn't seem very scary to me.

So yeah, I downvoted because what's going on just didn't grip me, probably because I spent most of my time trying to figure out what was going on, "make you a grammar nazi" didn't work as a hook for me, etc. etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woohoo! Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Taken under the lens of "first SCP", this is great. I upvoted for grammar-based language SCP anyway. It could use some ironing, but really, this is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I PMed you both.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see the interview log, and I didn't think it at all odd that they'd have D-class for research on a cognitohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should definitely add that. Litboners are definitely prevalent in SCP-1390-2 subjects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I'm having second thoughts about it. It's kind of… almost cartoonish. And if D-Class Researchers pissed people off, I think a character in the log will just result in people's heads exploding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Memtics are tic-tacs that affect your memory; orange ones make memories from your youth very vivid, green ones make you recall high school and early college clear as day, and the white ones clear your memory entirely excepting the recollection that you are at peace and content with your existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Attached the interview log!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh geeze, an angelic grammar nazi language. I think I popped a literary boner, which should be added as an obvious sign of stage 1 infection.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… you made an entire language that's a cognitohazard SCP. That turns people into the ultimate grammar nazis. This. Is fucking. Awesome.
And this is seriously your first attempt?

… DUDE. Please tell me you have more like this. Upvoted so damned hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, soul, that particular redaction is good here, at least in my opinion. It does what it's supposed to do, and I have a pretty good idea of what's behind it. Chill.

Also, I think this is pretty neat. UV'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this . Interesting take on the concept. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, it's there. I'm serious, you can pretty easily figure out the basics of stage three with what happens under the Level 4 Security Clearance thing. Sorry if it got you that mad, man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The redaction of stage 3 pisses me the fuck off. Why would you have it in there if all you're going to do is cocktease us the whole tine?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually panicked last second and didn't tag it memetic. Should I add it?

Edit: Yeah, I initially thought so, but I managed to out-neurotic myself. I've seen a lot of people, especially first articles, use memetic real weird, so I decided to just kind of… drop it.

Edit 2: And yes, I did initially mistype memetic as "memtic" (which are anomalous tictacs and a different SCP entirely). I blame it being early.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may be the first case in which something that was actually memetic was marked as a mere cognitohazard. I feel all strange inside.

Edit: Check out the Tag FAQ and Understanding Memetics. I really think this is a memetic agent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote for now. Concept is alright, and you did the expungment pretty well. No overall complaints, just meh. Might retread some of it later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I'm ever going to like a language SCP, but I think this is the only one I haven't disliked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said, I tried to make sure that everything REDACTED was actually answered in the Addenda or an incident. Were there any you felt were not answered well enough? Because that's definitely a failure of the SCP, in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The rest of the execution was enough to prevent me downvoting, but the redation here is extremely frustrating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ha, I like that! It's an interesting theory. Really, my whole idea in writing it was trying to play with the commonly-linked idea of language and magic, except I wanted to mostly explore the magician and the linguist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Easy way to test that.
"Hey 343! Do you recognize this?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. +1.

Also, in my headcanon i kind of took it as is, and figured it is a language legitimately given to man by god, like the purest form of words. Unfortuanetly this threatens the normalcy of the universe so the Foundation contained it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not aliens. And it doesn't mess with your language skills, the way I see it. The SCP really does have a "perfect" grammar. Its effects, however, create some weird stuff.

Edit: Of course, I can see how people would assume that it is an alien grammar. I didn't outright say it wasn't because I thought it was a pretty good answer and didn't necessitate a downvote. But in my headcanon (yes I have a headcanon of my own article) it's not caused by aliens but something much more mundane.

Edito Dos: And I think I culled anything that could make people think it's aliens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is too many existing concepts mashed together. We've already got ancient aliens, forbidden languages, SCPs that mess with your language skills, etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I considered that, but it seemed like a real mouthful! Thanks for the help and pointing out how dumb D-Class Researchers looked when typed out. I dunno how I thought it would fly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This means a lot to me. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it helps, I tried to make every REDACTED revealed by the end of the document,

Oh, huh, I hadn't noticed that. Nevermind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it helps, I tried to make every REDACTED revealed by the end of the document, or at least easily conjectured. Were there any that really made you grind your teeth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few to many [REDACTED]s for my taste, but otherwise interesting. I'm intrigued by the notion of a culture where xenophobia is integrated into the very grammar of the language.

EDIT: Also, MTF Epsilon-13 (aka "Grammar Police") made me laugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, as-is , even.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! And also, I took you up on your invitation and PMed you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D-Class translators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D-Class who perform research because they're infected…
